title: CPU, RAM, and Storage Drive in context
objective: Kick off the week's topic and build the right vocabulary.
week_focus: A+ Core 1: hardware, boot process, and internal components
checkpoint: Can explain what each main computer component does and how a system boots.
plain_language_intro: Today covers CPU, RAM, and Storage Drive within a+ core 1: hardware, boot process, and internal components.
mini_lecture_markdown:
The week focus is A+ Core 1: hardware, boot process, and internal components, and today's objective is kick off the week's topic and build the right vocabulary. The main terms in scope are CPU, RAM, and Storage Drive. The focus is on what each one does, how it operates, and how it affects the rest of the system.

CPU is central Processing Unit, executes instructions and coordinates processing tasks. It fetches, decodes, and executes instructions while coordinating arithmetic, logic, and control operations across the system. Mechanically, it fetches instructions, decodes them, and executes operations while registers and caches keep data close to processing cores. RAM is volatile memory used for active processes and short-term data access. It holds active instructions and data in fast volatile memory for immediate CPU access during execution. Mechanically, it holds active instructions and data in fast volatile cells that the CPU can read or update quickly. Storage Drive is persistent storage device such as HDD or SSD for operating system and files. It persists operating system files and user data by writing blocks to magnetic or solid-state media. Mechanically, it writes data into addressable sectors or cells, then retrieves it later through the controller and filesystem mapping. Together, these terms describe where work happens, how state changes, and what the visible outcome looks like when the system is operating normally.

The task list for today is practical by design. Watch the A+ course intro and the CPU / RAM / storage basics. Start a glossary for hardware terms you will reuse all month.

Resources such as Professor Messer A+ Core 1 (220-1201), LearnFree Computers 101, and CPU Features provide concrete examples for this topic. In this week, A+ Core 1: hardware, boot process, and internal components is easier to understand when the examples are tied to visible behavior and concrete outputs.

In a scenario, CPU, RAM, and Storage Drive usually matters through symptoms, sequence, or visible system behavior rather than through a direct definition prompt.
exam_relevance_markdown: This material is most likely to appear as a short scenario where you must identify how CPU, RAM, and Storage Drive affect the outcome. That can mean choosing the best answer for a symptom, distinguishing two similar terms, following a process step in the right order, or recognizing why one configuration works while another fails. The exam signal is applied reasoning: knowing what the concept does in context and what evidence points to it.
key_distinctions: CPU vs RAM — CPU: Central Processing Unit, executes instructions and coordinates processing tasks. RAM: Volatile memory used for active processes and short-term data access.
RAM vs Storage Drive — RAM: Volatile memory used for active processes and short-term data access. Storage Drive: Persistent storage device such as HDD or SSD for operating system and files.
Storage Drive vs RAM — Storage Drive: Persistent storage device such as HDD or SSD for operating system and files. RAM: Volatile memory used for active processes and short-term data access.
Recognition vs diagnosis — knowing the term name is not enough; know what evidence would make you pick it in a scenario.
mental_model_markdown: Use a pipeline model for this day: input arrives, a component or service processes it, state changes occur, and an output or symptom becomes visible to the user, administrator, or analyst. CPU: fetches instructions, decodes them, and executes operations while registers and caches keep data close to processing cores RAM: holds active instructions and data in fast volatile cells that the CPU can read or update quickly Storage Drive: writes data into addressable sectors or cells, then retrieves it later through the controller and filesystem mapping That sequence is the main mental model for the topic.
self_check_prompts: Explain the difference between CPU and RAM without using the words "better" or "faster" as your whole answer.
Describe what CPU does, what input it works on, and what evidence would show that it is functioning correctly.
Walk through today's objective in sequence and say which step would fail first if the system were misconfigured.
Connect today's glossary terms to this week's deliverable: what part of the artifact proves that you understand the mechanism and not just the label?
Give one realistic troubleshooting or security scenario where today's concepts would change the next decision you make.
quick_recap: Today covered CPU, RAM, and Storage Drive in terms of role, mechanism, and system effect.
